subject: Chemistry
solution:
Reduction half reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+6 e^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
Oxidation half reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 e^{-}
$$
Overall reaction :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+\mathrm{Cr}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+3 e^{-} \\
& 3 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
$3 \mathrm{~F}$ of electricity $=3$ moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$
$1 \mathrm{~F}$ of electricity $=\frac{3}{3}$ moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The standard reduction potential for a metal/metal insoluble salt electrode is related to the standard reduction potential of the metal ion and the solubility product $K_{sp}$ of the salt.
The half-cell reaction for the $X^-/MX(s)/M$ electrode is: $MX(s) + e^- \rightarrow M(s) + X^-(aq)$.
This can be viewed as a combination of two processes:
1. $MX(s) \rightleftharpoons M^+(aq) + X^-(aq)$ with equilibrium constant $K_{sp} = 10^{-10}$
2. $M^+(aq) + e^- \rightarrow M(s)$ with $E^\ominus_{M^+/M} = 0.79\text{ V}$
The relation between the standard potentials is given by:
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = E^\ominus_{M^+/M} + \frac{2.303RT}{nF} \log_{10} K_{sp}$
Given $n = 1$ and $\frac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.059\text{ V}$:
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 + 0.059 \log_{10}(10^{-10})$
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 + 0.059(-10)$
$E^\ominus_{X^-/MX/M} = 0.79 - 0.59 = 0.20\text{ V}$
To convert the value into mV:
$0.20\text{ V} = 0.20 \times 1000\text{ mV} = 200\text{ mV}$

For the reduction of H2O at cathode,

2H2O+2e-→H2g+2OH-; Ered∘=-1.23V

For Ca2++2e-→Ca; Ered∘=-2.87V

i.e., water will be reduced at the cathode, in preference to the liberation of Ca.

However, in the case of Ag, Cu and Cr, their standard reduction potentials are higher than -1.23 V and will be liberated/ deposited at the cathode and H2O will not be reduced.

Since E° cell is an intensive property, the E° cell values are not additive by nature. But the standard Gibbs free energy change is an extensive property and hence can be added.

(i) Fe+3→Fe+3e-→Z=E1o;  ΔG1=-3×F×z

(ii) Fe+2→Fe+2e- Y=E2o;  ΔG2=-2×F×y

Subtracting (ii) from (i)
Fe+3→Fe+2 ΔG3=ΔG1-ΔG2

Eo=-2y+3z

=x+2y-3z

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The half-reactions and their standard Gibbs free energy changes are given by:

$Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Fe$

$\Delta G^{\ominus}_{1} = -2FX$

$Fe^{3+} + 3e^{-} \rightarrow Fe$

$\Delta G^{\ominus}_{2} = -3FY$

The required half-reaction is:

$Fe^{3+} + e^{-} \rightarrow Fe^{2+}$

$\Delta G^{\ominus}_{3} = -1FE^{\ominus}_{Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}}$

This reaction is obtained by subtracting the first reaction from the second reaction:

$\Delta G^{\ominus}_{3} = \Delta G^{\ominus}_{2} - \Delta G^{\ominus}_{1}$

$-FE^{\ominus}_{Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}} = -3FY - (-2FX)$

$-FE^{\ominus}_{Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}} = -3FY + 2FX$

$E^{\ominus}_{Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}} = 3Y - 2X$

Answer: $3Y-2X$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The relation between Gibbs Free energy and emf of the cell can be related as follows,

∆G=-nFEcell

The electrode reactions can be written as follows,

Pb2++2e-→Pb       ∆G1=-2FEPb2+/Pbo

Pb4++4e-→Pb       ∆G2=-4FEPb4+/Pbo

Pb4++2e-→Pb2+       ∆G3=-2FEPb4+/Pb2+o

Now, ∆G3=∆G2-∆G1

-2FEPb4+/Pb2+o=-4FEPb4+/Pbo+2FEPb2+/Pbo

⇒4EPb+4/Pbo=2EPb+2/Pbo+2EPb+4/Pb+2o

⇒4n=2m+2EPb+4/Pb+2o⇒EPb+4/Pb+2o=2n-m⇒EPb+2/Pb+4o=m-2n

Hence, the value of x=2

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The cell Ag(s)|AgCl(s)|FeCl₂(aq), FeCl₃(aq)|Pt(s) has the reaction:
Ag(s) + Cl⁻ + Fe³⁺ → AgCl(s) + Fe²⁺
Using Nernst equation:
$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.059}{n}\log Q$ where $Q = \frac{[Fe^{2+}]}{[Fe^{3+}][Cl^-]}$
For $E_{cell}$ to increase, Q must decrease. Analyzing each condition:
A. Increase [Fe²⁺] increases Q, decreases $E_{cell}$ - false
B. Decrease [Fe³⁺] increases Q (smaller denominator), decreases $E_{cell}$ - false
C. Decrease [Fe²⁺] decreases Q, increases $E_{cell}$ - true
D. Increase [Fe³⁺] decreases Q, increases $E_{cell}$ - true
E. Increase [Cl⁻] decreases Q, increases $E_{cell}$ - true
Therefore C, D, and E cause $E_{cell}$ to increase.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \mathrm{Q} \\
& \text { Where } \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}
\end{aligned}$
For complete discharge $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0$
So $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right|=10^{37.3}$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Brine solution is the concentrated solution of NaCl in water.

 NaCl (aq.)→Na(aq) ++Cl(aq) +

The cathode will attract the sodium ions from the sodium chloride and hydrogen ions from the water. The hydrogen ions gain 2 electrons and are reduced to form hydrogen molecules.

2H+ (aq) + 2e- → H2 (g)

2H2O + 2e− → H2 (g) + 2OH− (aq)

The anode will attract the hydroxide ions from the water and chlorine ions from sodium chloride. The chlorine ions lose 2 electrons and get oxidised.

Cl2 → 2Cl− + 2e−

Na+ + OH− → NaOH

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Conductivity changes with the concentration of electrolyte. The number of ions per unit volume carrying the current decreases on dilution, so conductivity (K) always decreases with decrease in concentration.
Molar. Conductivity increases with dilution.
∧mmolar conductivity=1000×KC

Cds+2NiOH3→CdOs+2NiOH2s+H2Ol

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Species having the highest reduction potential i.e., tendency to undergo reduction by gain of electron. SRP value increases from left to right in the period for the d-block elements.
M 3+ (aq) +1 e − →  M 2+ (aq)
26Fe3+/Fe2+⇒4s03d5→4s03d6+0.77 volt27Co3+/Co2+⇒4s03d6→4s03d7+1.97 volt24Cr3+/Cr2+4s03d3→4s03d4-0.4 volt25Mn3+/Mn2+4s03d4→4s03d5+1.57 volt

Highest reduction potential means strong oxidising agent, i.e. undergoing reduction Co3++1e-→Co2+.
